enrichment.short_description: Captivate is a podcast hosting, analytics, and monetization platform offering unlimited storage, IAB-certified analytics, and creator tools starting at $19/month.
enrichment.seo_title: Captivate Review: Podcast Hosting Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Captivate.fm podcast hosting review: pricing plans, IAB-certified analytics, monetization tools, and creator features. Plans start at $19/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Captivate (Captivate.fm) is a podcast hosting and monetization platform founded in January 2019 by Mark Asquith and Kieran McKeefery in Sheffield, United Kingdom. In December 2021, UK media company Global (Global Media & Entertainment, owner of Capital, Heart, Classic FM, and LBC) acquired Captivate, folding the podcast-hosting startup into its portfolio while keeping it running as a standalone platform for independent podcasters. Captivate hosts podcast audio files, generates RSS feeds, and distributes episodes to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and other listening apps, while providing IAB-certified analytics that meet the Interactive Advertising Bureau's measurement standard used by advertisers to buy podcast ad inventory. The platform runs on three paid plans — Personal at $19/month ($204/year) for up to 30,000 monthly downloads, Professional at $49/month ($528/year) for up to 150,000 monthly downloads, and Business at $99/month ($1,080/year) for up to 300,000 monthly downloads — with every plan receiving the identical full feature set; the only difference between tiers is the monthly download allowance. All plans include a 30-day free trial and unlimited storage, so creators are never charged extra for uploading more episodes, only for downloads beyond their plan's cap. Distinctive features include dynamic ad insertion, which lets ads be swapped into older episodes without re-uploading audio; a built-in podcast website generated automatically for each show; guest booking and scheduling tools; and private podcasting for members-only or internal audio feeds. Captivate bundles what it calls the Captivate Creator Suite into every plan at no extra cost: email opt-in forms for building a listener mailing list, membership and tipping tools for direct listener monetization, and AI-assisted show notes generation. The platform also offers Podcast Growth Labs, an education resource aimed at helping hosts grow audience and revenue. Analytics on every plan include performance comparison across episodes, listener location/device/app breakdowns, unique listener counts for measuring retention, and web player consumption analytics. Captivate exposes a public REST API (documented at docs.captivate.fm) that lets developers authenticate, manage shows and RSS feeds, create and update episodes, upload media, and pull analytics data, plus webhook support for automation. Captivate positions itself against competitors like Buzzsprout, Libsyn, and Podbean by not gating core features behind higher tiers — the download cap is the only lever, which simplifies plan selection as a show's audience grows. Because Captivate has no permanently free plan (only a 30-day trial), it best suits podcasters already committed to publishing regularly rather than casual hobbyists testing the medium. The service covers the full podcast production and monetization lifecycle short of audio editing itself: hosting, distribution, analytics, ad monetization, and audience growth tools. Its ownership by a major European radio and media group also gives it potential access to Global's advertiser relationships for ad monetization, differentiating it from independently owned hosting platforms. Captivate is delivered as a web application with a browser-based dashboard rather than a native desktop or mobile app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Captivate is a podcast hosting, analytics, and monetization platform founded in 2019 by Mark Asquith and Kieran McKeefery in Sheffield, United Kingdom. It was acquired by UK media group Global in December 2021 and continues to operate as a dedicated platform for independent podcasters rather than being merged into Global's own broadcast properties.

The platform handles the core mechanics of podcasting — audio hosting, RSS feed generation, and distribution to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and other apps — while layering on IAB-certified analytics, monetization tooling, and audience growth features that many rival hosts charge extra for or omit entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Captivate plan includes the same feature set: IAB-certified analytics, dynamic ad insertion, an auto-generated podcast website, guest booking tools, and private/members-only podcast feeds. Plans differ only by monthly download allowance, not by functionality.

The bundled Captivate Creator Suite adds email opt-in forms, listener membership and tipping tools, and AI-assisted show notes generation at no extra cost. A public REST API and webhook support let developers integrate hosting and analytics data into other tools and workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Captivate offers three tiers: Personal at $19/month for up to 30,000 monthly downloads, Professional at $49/month for up to 150,000 monthly downloads, and Business at $99/month for up to 300,000 monthly downloads. Annual billing brings modest savings ($204, $528, and $1,080 per year respectively).
There is no permanently free plan, but every tier includes a 30-day free trial with no feature restrictions, letting creators test the full platform before committing to a subscription.

features.title: IAB-Certified Analytics
features.description: Download and listener analytics that meet Interactive Advertising Bureau measurement standards, used by advertisers to value podcast ad inventory.

features.title: Dynamic Ad Insertion
features.description: Swap ads into current and back-catalog episodes without re-uploading audio files.

features.title: Built-in Podcast Website
features.description: An automatically generated, customizable website for each show, included on every plan.

features.title: Guest Booking & Scheduling
features.description: Tools for coordinating interview guests and managing recording schedules from within the platform.

features.title: Private Podcasting
features.description: Members-only or internal audio feeds for gated or non-public content.

features.title: Captivate Creator Suite
features.description: Bundled email opt-in forms, membership/tipping monetization, and AI-assisted show notes generation included free on all plans.

features.title: Unlimited Storage
features.description: No storage caps on any plan; billing is based only on monthly downloads.

features.title: Public API & Webhooks
features.description: REST API for managing shows, episodes, media, and analytics programmatically, plus webhook support for automation.

pros: No feature gating between tiers — every plan gets the same full toolset
Unlimited storage regardless of plan
IAB-certified analytics included even on the entry-level plan
Built-in monetization tools (ads, memberships, tipping) without third-party add-ons
30-day free trial with full feature access
cons: No permanently free plan, only a time-limited trial
Pricing scales with monthly downloads, so fast-growing shows must upgrade tiers
Fewer native mobile apps compared to some larger competitors
Dynamic ad monetization and sponsorship opportunities depend on reaching sufficient listener volume

faqs.question: How much does Captivate cost?
faqs.answer: Captivate has three paid plans: Personal at $19/month (up to 30,000 monthly downloads), Professional at $49/month (up to 150,000 monthly downloads), and Business at $99/month (up to 300,000 monthly downloads). Annual billing is also available at a discount.

faqs.question: Does Captivate have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Captivate does not offer a permanently free plan, but every paid plan includes a 30-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card charge until the trial ends.

faqs.question: Is storage limited on Captivate?
faqs.answer: No. Storage is unlimited on every plan; Captivate bills based on monthly downloads rather than the amount of audio uploaded.

faqs.question: Who owns Captivate?
faqs.answer: Captivate was founded independently in 2019 and acquired by Global (Global Media & Entertainment), a major UK radio and media company, in December 2021.

faqs.question: Does Captivate offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes. Captivate provides a public REST API and webhook support for managing shows, episodes, media uploads, and analytics data programmatically.

faqs.question: Can I monetize my podcast on Captivate?
faqs.answer: Yes. Captivate includes dynamic ad insertion, listener memberships, and tipping tools on every plan as part of its bundled Creator Suite.

use_cases.title: Independent podcast hosting
use_cases.description: Hosting, RSS distribution, and IAB-certified analytics for shows publishing regularly across major listening apps.
use_cases.audience: Independent podcasters and small podcast networks

use_cases.title: Podcast monetization
use_cases.description: Dynamic ad insertion, listener memberships, and tipping to generate revenue directly from an existing audience.
use_cases.audience: Podcasters seeking to monetize an established show

use_cases.title: Private and internal podcasting
use_cases.description: Gated, members-only or internal-only audio feeds for training, community, or subscriber content.
use_cases.audience: Businesses and creators running private audio feeds

enrichment.short_description: CaptivateIQ is a no-code sales commission and incentive compensation management platform that automates commission calculations, statements, and plan design.
enrichment.seo_title: CaptivateIQ: Sales Commission Software Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CaptivateIQ is a no-code commission management platform for revenue teams. Explore its pricing model, key features, integrations, and best-fit use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CaptivateIQ is a sales commission and incentive compensation management (ICM) platform founded in 2017 by Mark Schopmeyer, Hubert Wong, and Conway Teng, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company has raised roughly $164.6 million in venture funding, including a $100 million Series C in January 2022 at a $1.25 billion valuation, from investors including Sequoia Capital, Accel, and ICONIQ Growth. CaptivateIQ employs approximately 260 people as of 2026. The product targets Revenue Operations, Finance, and Sales leadership teams that need to design, automate, and audit commission plans for sales organizations, replacing manual spreadsheet-based commission tracking with a purpose-built system. Its core differentiator is a no-code commission plan builder: RevOps and finance teams can construct complex, multi-tier commission structures — accelerators, tiered rates, split credit, SPIFs, and clawbacks — using a visual interface rather than requesting custom engineering or relying on rigid templates from legacy incentive compensation management vendors. The platform performs real-time commission calculations as deals close, generates commission statements that give sales reps transparency into how their pay is calculated, and includes a formal dispute resolution workflow so reps can flag discrepancies and have them tracked and resolved within the system rather than over email or Slack. Forecasting and what-if modeling tools let finance teams simulate the cost and behavioral impact of proposed plan changes before rolling them out. CaptivateIQ connects to CRM and data systems commonly used by revenue teams, including Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite, and data warehouses such as Snowflake, to pull deal and payout-relevant data automatically rather than requiring manual data entry. An audit trail records every change made to commission plans and calculations, which is important for SOC 2 compliance and for resolving disputes about historical payouts. CaptivateIQ does not publish standard pricing tiers; contracts are negotiated individually and priced primarily on a per-payee (per commissioned employee) basis, which is typical for enterprise incentive compensation software where implementation complexity scales with the number of commission plans, integrations, and payees involved. This makes CaptivateIQ best suited to mid-market and enterprise revenue organizations — typically dozens to hundreds of commissioned employees — rather than very small sales teams, since the value of automating complex, multi-plan commission structures increases with organizational scale and plan complexity. Competitors in the space include Xactly, Varicent, QuotaPath, and Spiff. CaptivateIQ differentiates primarily through its no-code plan design experience and its emphasis on a modern, self-serve UI for both admins configuring plans and reps checking their earnings, positioning it as an alternative to older, IT-dependent incentive compensation systems. The platform is delivered as a web application with API access for integration into a company's existing revenue tech stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CaptivateIQ is a sales commission management (incentive compensation management) platform founded in 2017 by Mark Schopmeyer, Hubert Wong, and Conway Teng, and headquartered in San Francisco. The company has raised approximately $164.6 million from investors including Sequoia, Accel, and ICONIQ Growth, reaching a $1.25 billion valuation in its 2022 Series C round.

CaptivateIQ replaces spreadsheet-based commission tracking with an automated system for calculating, communicating, and auditing sales commissions, aimed primarily at RevOps, Finance, and Sales leadership at mid-market and enterprise companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's signature feature is a no-code commission plan builder that lets non-engineers construct complex multi-tier commission structures, accelerators, and split-credit rules through a visual interface. Real-time calculations, rep-facing commission statements, and a formal dispute resolution workflow give both finance teams and sales reps visibility into how pay is derived.

Forecasting and what-if modeling let admins simulate the cost of proposed plan changes before launch, while integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite, and data warehouses like Snowflake pull deal data automatically. An audit trail tracks every change for compliance and dispute resolution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CaptivateIQ does not publish standard pricing tiers. Contracts are custom-quoted and priced primarily on a per-payee basis, reflecting the enterprise incentive compensation management category where implementation scope, integrations, and payee count all affect cost.

Because pricing is quote-based and scales with the number of commissioned employees, CaptivateIQ is generally positioned for mid-market and enterprise revenue organizations rather than very small sales teams. Prospective customers need to contact CaptivateIQ directly for a quote.

features.title: No-Code Commission Plan Builder
features.description: Visual interface for designing multi-tier commission structures, accelerators, splits, SPIFs, and clawbacks without engineering support.

features.title: Real-Time Commission Calculations
features.description: Commissions recalculate automatically as underlying deal and CRM data changes.

features.title: Rep Commission Statements
features.description: Transparent, self-serve statements that show sales reps exactly how their commission was calculated.

features.title: Dispute Resolution Workflow
features.description: A structured process for reps to flag and resolve commission discrepancies inside the platform.

features.title: Forecasting & What-If Modeling
features.description: Simulate the cost and impact of proposed commission plan changes before implementation.

features.title: CRM & Data Warehouse Integrations
features.description: Native connections to Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite, and data warehouses such as Snowflake.

features.title: Audit Trail
features.description: Full history of plan and calculation changes to support compliance and dispute resolution.

features.title: Approval Workflows
features.description: Configurable approval chains for commission plan changes and payout exceptions.

pros: No-code plan builder reduces dependence on engineering for commission plan changes
Handles complex, multi-tier, multi-plan commission structures
Strong native integrations with CRM, ERP, and data warehouse systems
Rep-facing statements and dispute workflow improve payout transparency and trust
Backed by well-established venture investors, suggesting long-term product investment
cons: No public pricing; all contracts require a custom sales quote
Enterprise-oriented implementation can take significant setup time
Best suited to mid-market/enterprise teams rather than very small sales organizations
Per-payee pricing can become costly as headcount and plan complexity grow

faqs.question: How much does CaptivateIQ cost?
faqs.answer: CaptivateIQ does not publish standard pricing. Contracts are custom-quoted, typically priced on a per-payee (per commissioned employee) basis, so cost depends on team size and plan complexity.

faqs.question: Who founded CaptivateIQ?
faqs.answer: CaptivateIQ was founded in 2017 by Mark Schopmeyer, Hubert Wong, and Conway Teng, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What CRMs does CaptivateIQ integrate with?
faqs.answer: CaptivateIQ integrates with Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite, and data warehouses such as Snowflake, among other revenue systems.

faqs.question: Is CaptivateIQ suitable for small sales teams?
faqs.answer: CaptivateIQ is primarily built for mid-market and enterprise revenue teams with more complex commission structures; very small sales teams may find simpler, lower-cost tools more cost-effective.

faqs.question: Does CaptivateIQ require coding to set up commission plans?
faqs.answer: No. CaptivateIQ's core feature is a no-code plan builder that lets RevOps and finance teams configure commission structures through a visual interface.

use_cases.title: Automating commission calculations
use_cases.description: Replacing spreadsheet-based commission tracking with automated, auditable calculations tied to CRM data.

use_cases.title: Designing complex incentive plans
use_cases.description: Building and testing multi-tier, multi-plan commission structures without engineering resources.

use_cases.title: Resolving commission disputes
use_cases.description: Giving sales reps transparent statements and a formal process to flag and resolve pay discrepancies.

enrichment.short_description: Carbonite is a cloud backup service, now part of OpenText, offering unlimited automatic backup for individuals and businesses with encryption and file versioning.
enrichment.seo_title: Carbonite Review: Cloud Backup Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Carbonite cloud backup pricing, plans, and features for home and business users, including unlimited storage, encryption, and automatic backup by OpenText.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Carbonite is a cloud backup service founded in 2005 and headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. In November 2019, enterprise information management company OpenText announced its acquisition of Carbonite for approximately $1.42–1.45 billion (about $23.00 per share), and the deal closed in December 2019, making Carbonite a wholly owned subsidiary within OpenText's cloud-based data protection, backup, disaster recovery, and endpoint security portfolio. Carbonite serves two main markets: personal/home users and small-to-midsize businesses. On the personal side, Carbonite Safe offers unlimited cloud storage for a single computer, with tiers differentiated mainly by add-on capabilities rather than storage caps — Safe Basic covers automatic backup of one computer, Safe Plus adds backup of one external hard drive, and Safe Prime adds video backup and access to Carbonite's Courier Recovery Service, a mail-in option where a physical hard drive containing a customer's backed-up data is shipped to them for large-scale restores rather than requiring a slow full download. On the business side, Carbonite Safe Pro/Server plans scale from Core (up to 25 computers, 250 GB base storage) to Power (adds one server, 500 GB base storage) to Ultimate (unlimited servers, 500 GB base storage), with additional storage purchasable in 100 GB increments. Core backup technology includes continuous and scheduled backup options, 256-bit encryption for data in transit and at rest, two-factor authentication for account security, and file versioning that retains the 12 most recent versions of a changed file so users can roll back accidental edits or deletions. Carbonite offers a 15-day free trial with no credit card required, letting prospective customers test backup functionality before subscribing; annual subscriptions are the standard billing model, with list starting prices around $59.99 for a one-year personal plan historically, though current per-month equivalent pricing across aggregator sources places the entry-level Safe Basic personal plan at roughly $4.91–$6.99 per month when billed annually. Carbonite differentiates itself from newer cloud backup entrants through its long operating history (founded in 2005, one of the earliest consumer cloud backup companies), its physical courier recovery option for large-scale restores, and its integration into OpenText's broader enterprise data protection and Microsoft 365 backup offerings for business customers. Language support is limited to English. Because personal plans cover only a single device (with optional external drive coverage on higher tiers), Carbonite is best suited to users who want straightforward, unlimited backup of one computer rather than multi-device household coverage, while business tiers are aimed at small IT teams needing to back up a defined number of workstations and servers. As part of OpenText, Carbonite benefits from enterprise-grade infrastructure and security practices, though OpenText does not break out Carbonite-specific employee counts publicly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Carbonite is a cloud backup service founded in 2005 and headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. It was one of the earliest consumer-facing cloud backup companies and has since expanded into small-business and server backup. OpenText acquired Carbonite for roughly $1.42–1.45 billion in a deal that closed in December 2019, and it now operates as part of OpenText's cloud data protection and endpoint security portfolio.

Carbonite serves both personal users backing up a single home computer and small businesses backing up workstations and servers, with plans differentiated primarily by device count and add-on recovery options rather than storage limits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Personal plans provide unlimited storage for one computer, with continuous or scheduled automatic backup, 256-bit encryption, and two-factor authentication for account security. File versioning retains the 12 most recent versions of a file, allowing rollback of accidental changes or deletions.

Higher personal tiers add external hard drive backup and the Courier Recovery Service, a mail-in physical drive option for restoring large amounts of data without a lengthy download. Business plans (Safe Pro) scale from 25-computer coverage up to unlimited servers with 100 GB storage add-on blocks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Carbonite's personal plans start around $4.91–$6.99 per month when billed annually for Safe Basic, scaling up through Safe Plus and Safe Prime for external drive and courier recovery features. Business Safe Pro/Server plans range from roughly $24/month for Core (25 computers) to $83/month for Ultimate (unlimited servers).

A 15-day free trial with no credit card required is available for prospective customers to test backup functionality before subscribing. Billing is primarily annual, and additional storage for business plans is sold in 100 GB blocks.

features.title: Unlimited Storage (Personal)
features.description: No cap on backed-up data volume for a single covered computer on personal plans.

features.title: Automatic Continuous & Scheduled Backup
features.description: Files back up continuously or on a set schedule, with the ability to exclude certain hours of the day.

features.title: 256-bit Encryption
features.description: Data is encrypted both in transit and at rest to protect backed-up files.

features.title: File Versioning
features.description: Retains the 12 most recent versions of a changed file for rollback purposes.

features.title: Two-Factor Authentication
features.description: Optional SMS-based verification code required when logging in from a new device.

features.title: Courier Recovery Service
features.description: Mail-in physical hard drive recovery option for restoring large volumes of data (Safe Prime and business tiers).

features.title: External Hard Drive Backup
features.description: Backup coverage for one external hard drive in addition to the primary computer (Plus/Prime tiers).

features.title: Server & Microsoft 365 Backup
features.description: Business tiers extend coverage to physical/virtual servers and, through OpenText's broader portfolio, Microsoft 365 data.

pros: Unlimited storage on personal plans regardless of data volume
Long operating history and enterprise backing from OpenText
Physical courier recovery option for restoring large data sets without a slow download
256-bit encryption and two-factor authentication for account security
cons: Personal plans cover only a single computer by default
English-only language support
File version history is capped at the 12 most recent versions rather than unlimited
Business/server plans are priced and licensed separately, which can add up at scale

faqs.question: How much does Carbonite cost?
faqs.answer: Personal plans start at roughly $4.91–$6.99/month when billed annually for the Safe Basic tier, with Plus and Prime tiers costing more for added recovery options. Business Safe Pro plans range from about $24/month to $83/month depending on the number of computers and servers covered.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Carbonite?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carbonite offers a 15-day free trial that does not require a credit card, allowing users to test backup functionality before subscribing.

faqs.question: Who owns Carbonite now?
faqs.answer: Carbonite is a wholly owned subsidiary of OpenText, which acquired the company for approximately $1.42–1.45 billion in a deal that closed in December 2019.

faqs.question: Does Carbonite back up more than one computer?
faqs.answer: Personal plans cover a single computer, with higher tiers adding coverage for one external hard drive. Business Safe Pro plans support multiple computers and servers depending on tier.

faqs.question: How many file versions does Carbonite keep?
faqs.answer: Carbonite retains the 12 most recent versions of a changed file, allowing users to restore an earlier version if needed.

faqs.question: Does Carbonite support two-factor authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carbonite offers optional SMS-based two-factor authentication when logging in from a new device.

use_cases.title: Personal computer backup
use_cases.description: Unlimited, automatic backup of a single home computer with optional external drive coverage.

use_cases.title: Small business workstation and server backup
use_cases.description: Backup coverage for a defined number of business computers and servers, with expandable storage.

use_cases.title: Large-scale data recovery
use_cases.description: Physical courier recovery service for restoring large volumes of backed-up data without a slow download.
use_cases.audience: Users recovering from major data loss or hardware failure

enrichment.short_description: Carepatron is an all-in-one practice management platform for health and wellness practitioners, combining scheduling, telehealth, billing, and AI notes.
enrichment.seo_title: Carepatron: Practice Management Software Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Carepatron is a practice management platform for health and wellness practitioners with scheduling, telehealth, billing, and AI notes. Free plan available.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Carepatron is an all-in-one practice management platform for health and wellness practitioners, founded in 2019 by CEO Jamie Frew and CTO David Pene in Tauranga, New Zealand. The platform officially launched in 2021 with a fuller release in early 2022. Carepatron has raised approximately $5 million in funding from investors including Blackbird Ventures and TQ Ventures, and reports serving more than 10,000 healthcare teams and over 100,000 clinicians across 120+ countries, with its interface translated into roughly 24 languages. The company employs an estimated 40–50 people, a lean team relative to its stated user base. Carepatron's product consolidates several functions that many clinics otherwise buy as separate tools: appointment scheduling with automated reminders, telehealth video consultations, a client-facing patient portal, clinical documentation and customizable care plan/note templates, billing and invoicing, and an AI-powered medical scribe that transcribes and structures session notes automatically. The platform is positioned for solo practitioners and small-to-midsize health and wellness practices — including therapists, counselors, physical therapists, and other allied health professionals — rather than large hospital systems. Security and compliance are emphasized throughout the product: Carepatron states it maintains end-to-end encryption, SOC 2 certification, GDPR compliance for international/EU clients, and HIPAA compliance for US healthcare data by default across its plans. Pricing follows a freemium model. The Free plan provides core scheduling, telehealth, and documentation functionality suited to solo or very small practices at no cost. Paid tiers are Essential at $29/month, Plus at $39/month, and Advanced at $49/month, each progressively adding capacity such as unlimited storage, deeper AI workflow automation, custom branding, group scheduling, and analytics; some sources cite an Organization-level enterprise tier priced around $1,990/month for larger, multi-location organizations needing custom configuration and dedicated support, though enterprise pricing is generally quote-based rather than fixed. Carepatron ships with native iOS and Android mobile apps in addition to its web application, letting practitioners manage scheduling and documentation from mobile devices. Competitors in the practice management and EHR space include SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, and Practice Fusion; Carepatron differentiates primarily through its free entry tier, bundled AI scribe functionality even at lower price points, and broad international/multi-language support, positioning it as an accessible option for practitioners and small clinics that find legacy EHR systems costly or complex to adopt. As a company founded in 2019 and only broadly launched in 2021–2022, Carepatron is younger than many established EHR and practice management vendors, though its combination of compliance certifications, AI features, and free-tier accessibility has helped it grow quickly within the allied health and wellness segment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Carepatron is a practice management platform for health and wellness practitioners, founded in 2019 by Jamie Frew (CEO) and David Pene (CTO) in Tauranga, New Zealand. It launched publicly in 2021 with a fuller release in early 2022 and has since grown to serve more than 10,000 healthcare teams and 100,000+ clinicians across over 120 countries.

The platform consolidates scheduling, telehealth, documentation, billing, and an AI-powered clinical scribe into a single product, aiming to replace the patchwork of separate tools that many small practices otherwise rely on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality includes appointment scheduling with automated reminders, telehealth video consultations, a client-facing patient portal, and customizable clinical documentation and care plan templates. An AI medical scribe transcribes and structures session notes, reducing manual documentation time.

Carepatron emphasizes compliance and security, citing end-to-end encryption, SOC 2 certification, GDPR compliance, and HIPAA adherence across its plans, along with native iOS and Android mobile apps alongside the web application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Carepatron offers a free plan covering core scheduling, telehealth, and documentation for solo or very small practices. Paid tiers are Essential at $29/month, Plus at $39/month, and Advanced at $49/month, adding unlimited storage, deeper AI workflow automation, custom branding, group scheduling, and analytics.
Larger organizations needing custom configuration and dedicated support can access an enterprise-level Organization plan, which is generally quote-based rather than fixed-price.

features.title: Scheduling & Reminders
features.description: Appointment booking with automated client reminders to reduce no-shows.

features.title: Telehealth
features.description: Built-in video consultations for remote client sessions.

features.title: Client Portal
features.description: Secure, client-facing portal for intake forms, messaging, and document sharing.

features.title: AI Medical Scribe
features.description: AI-generated session notes and documentation to reduce manual charting time.

features.title: Billing & Invoicing
features.description: Integrated invoicing and payment collection tied to appointments and services.

features.title: Customizable Templates & Care Plans
features.description: Editable clinical note, intake, and care plan templates across specialties.

features.title: Compliance Certifications
features.description: End-to-end encryption with SOC 2, GDPR, and HIPAA compliance across plans.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps in addition to the web platform.

pros: Genuinely usable free plan for solo practitioners
AI scribe included even on lower-priced tiers
Broad compliance coverage (HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2) built into the product
Supports practitioners across 120+ countries with multi-language interface
Consolidates scheduling, billing, telehealth, and documentation into one platform
cons: Advanced analytics, branding, and automation are reserved for higher-priced tiers
Enterprise Organization pricing requires a custom quote rather than being published
Younger company (founded 2019) than many established EHR vendors
Template and feature depth can vary by clinical specialty

faqs.question: Does Carepatron have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carepatron offers a free plan with core scheduling, telehealth, and documentation features suited to solo or very small practices.

faqs.question: How much does Carepatron cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $29/month for the Essential tier, with Plus at $39/month and Advanced at $49/month. An enterprise Organization tier is available with custom, quote-based pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Is Carepatron HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carepatron states it maintains HIPAA compliance for US healthcare data, alongside GDPR compliance and SOC 2 certification.

faqs.question: Does Carepatron include an AI scribe?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carepatron includes an AI-powered medical scribe that transcribes and structures clinical session notes automatically.

faqs.question: Who founded Carepatron?
faqs.answer: Carepatron was founded in 2019 by CEO Jamie Frew and CTO David Pene in Tauranga, New Zealand.

faqs.question: Does Carepatron have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carepatron offers native iOS and Android apps in addition to its web-based platform.

use_cases.title: Solo practice management
use_cases.description: Scheduling, telehealth, billing, and documentation for a single practitioner using the free or entry-level paid plan.
use_cases.audience: Solo therapists, counselors, and allied health practitioners

use_cases.title: Multi-practitioner clinic operations
use_cases.description: Group scheduling, custom branding, and analytics for growing clinics with multiple providers.

use_cases.title: Compliant clinical documentation
use_cases.description: HIPAA/GDPR-compliant note-taking and care plans with AI-assisted transcription.

enrichment.short_description: Carrd is a simple, free-to-start builder for responsive one-page websites, with paid Pro plans starting at just $9/year for extra sites and features.
enrichment.seo_title: Carrd Review: One-Page Website Builder & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Carrd is a simple one-page website builder with drag-and-drop editing and responsive design. Free plan available; Pro plans start at $9/year.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Carrd is a website builder specialized in simple, responsive one-page sites, created by a solo developer known online as AJ, who is based in Nashville, Tennessee. AJ originally conceived the idea in 2015 as an offshoot of his freelance web design and template work; he publicly launched Carrd via Twitter on March 7, 2016, followed by a Product Hunt launch on March 16, 2016, which drove its early growth. Unlike most website builders, Carrd requires no account to start building — users can drag, drop, and edit a live site directly in the browser, and only need to create an account when they're ready to publish, which effectively turns the product into its own demo. The tool is deliberately narrow in scope: it is built for single-page sites such as portfolios, landing pages, link-in-bio pages, and pre-launch pages, rather than full multi-page websites, blogs, or e-commerce stores. Every Carrd site is responsive by default, automatically resizing and restacking content for phones, tablets, and desktops without additional configuration. The product supports embedding third-party widgets and services, including Stripe and PayPal for payments, Typeform for forms, and Mailchimp and Google Analytics for marketing and tracking, letting a one-page site handle transactional and lead-generation tasks despite its simplicity. Carrd's pricing is notable for being both freemium and extremely inexpensive: the Free tier allows up to 3 sites with Carrd branding and core building tools, while paid Pro tiers are billed annually rather than monthly — Pro Lite at $9/year, Pro Standard at $19/year (adding custom forms, Google Analytics integration, and more widget embeds), and Pro Plus at $49/year (adding password protection, file download functionality, and support for up to 25 sites). This pricing strategy, combined with word-of-mouth and Product Hunt-driven growth, helped Carrd scale to what the founder has described as over 800,000 users and more than 1.2 to 4 million sites built on the platform, generating six-figure monthly recurring revenue while operated by a team of essentially two people — AJ and a collaborator — rather than a venture-backed startup. Carrd is frequently used by indie developers, designers, and small businesses that need a fast, credible web presence without hiring a developer or learning a page builder with a steep learning curve like WordPress or Webflow. Its constraints — single-page format, limited customization ceiling relative to code-based builders, and a small support team — are the tradeoff for its speed, affordability, and simplicity. Language support is English, and there is no native mobile app; Carrd is a web-based, browser-first tool for both building and viewing sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Carrd is a website builder focused exclusively on simple, responsive one-page sites. It was created by a solo developer known as AJ, based in Nashville, Tennessee, who conceived the idea in 2015 and launched it publicly via Twitter and Product Hunt in March 2016.

Unlike most page builders, Carrd requires no account to start: users can build and preview a live site directly in the browser and only sign up when ready to publish. The product has grown into a widely used indie tool, reportedly powering well over a million sites while being run by a team of essentially two people.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Carrd site is responsive by default, automatically adapting layout for phones, tablets, and desktops. The drag-and-drop editor supports embedding third-party widgets and services, including Stripe and PayPal for payments, Typeform for forms, and Mailchimp and Google Analytics for marketing and tracking.

Higher tiers add custom forms, password-protected pages, file downloads, and support for managing more sites under one account, but the core building experience remains consistent across free and paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Carrd's Free plan allows up to 3 sites with core building tools and Carrd branding. Paid Pro tiers are billed annually at notably low prices: Pro Lite at $9/year, Pro Standard at $19/year (adds custom forms, Google Analytics, and more widget embeds), and Pro Plus at $49/year (adds password protection, downloads, and up to 25 sites).

This annual, low-cost pricing model is a deliberate strategy that has helped Carrd achieve broad adoption among indie developers, designers, and small businesses without requiring a large sales or marketing operation.

features.description: Gate pages behind a password and offer file downloads (Pro Plus tier).

No account needed to start building — the editor itself is the demo
Responsive design out of the box with no extra setup
Very fast to launch a simple, credible web presence
Large community and template ecosystem despite the tiny team behind it
cons: Limited to one-page/simple sites, not suited for multi-page or complex sites
Fewer built-in blogging and e-commerce features than full website builders
Customization ceiling is lower than code-based site builders
Support is limited given the very small team running the product

faqs.answer: Yes, Carrd's Free plan allows up to 3 sites with core building tools. Paid Pro tiers, starting at $9/year, add more sites and features like custom forms and password protection.

faqs.question: Who created Carrd?
faqs.answer: Carrd was created by a solo developer known online as AJ, based in Nashville, Tennessee, who launched it publicly in March 2016.

faqs.question: Can I build a multi-page website with Carrd?
faqs.answer: No, Carrd is purpose-built for single-page sites such as portfolios, landing pages, and link-in-bio pages rather than full multi-page websites.

faqs.question: Do Carrd sites work on mobile?
faqs.answer: Yes, every Carrd site is responsive by default and automatically adapts its layout for phones, tablets, and desktops.

faqs.question: Does Carrd support payments and forms?
faqs.answer: Yes, Carrd supports embedding third-party services like Stripe and PayPal for payments and Typeform for custom forms.

faqs.question: How is Carrd priced?
faqs.answer: Carrd uses annual billing: Free (3 sites), Pro Lite at $9/year, Pro Standard at $19/year, and Pro Plus at $49/year (up to 25 sites).

enrichment.short_description: CasaOS is a free, open-source personal cloud operating system with a one-click app store, letting anyone turn a Raspberry Pi or old PC into a home server.
enrichment.seo_title: CasaOS: Open-Source Personal Cloud Server Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CasaOS is a free, open-source personal cloud OS with a one-click app store for Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Home Assistant, and more. Runs on Raspberry Pi to x86.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CasaOS is a free, open-source personal cloud operating system originally developed by IceWhale Technology, a company founded in 2020 and based in Shanghai, China, with additional operations noted in Hong Kong and Shenzhen. CasaOS was created as the software layer for ZimaBoard, a crowdfunded x86 single-board server that IceWhale launched on Kickstarter; the OS was subsequently released as its own open-source project and has since grown independently of the hardware, running on a wide range of devices from a $35 Raspberry Pi up to repurposed laptops and dedicated servers. The project is hosted on GitHub under the IceWhaleTech organization and has accumulated more than 33,000 GitHub stars, making it one of the most popular home-server dashboard projects by that measure, alongside competitors like Umbrel, TrueNAS, and OpenMediaVault. CasaOS is not itself an operating system installed from scratch; it runs on top of an existing Linux installation such as Ubuntu, Debian, or Raspberry Pi OS, layering a clean, modern web-based dashboard over the underlying system for managing apps, files, and storage. Its defining feature is a one-click app store containing 100-plus community-curated applications — including Nextcloud for file sync, Jellyfin for media streaming, Home Assistant for home automation, Gitea for self-hosted git repositories, and AdGuard for network-wide ad blocking — each packaged as a standardized 'CasaOS app' combining a JSON manifest with a Docker Compose definition. Under the hood, a Go-based service called CasaOS-AppManagement translates dashboard clicks into `docker compose up` commands, meaning every installed application runs in its own isolated Docker container. Beyond the app store, CasaOS provides a built-in web file browser for navigating and managing files without SSH access, and storage management tools that visualize disk usage and let users mount external drives directly from the dashboard. Because it is fully open-source and free, CasaOS has no pricing tiers, subscriptions, or paid features; it is licensed for self-hosted deployment by individuals or organizations comfortable managing their own Linux server. Development is community-driven, with IceWhale Technology maintaining the core project while community contributors add and maintain individual app store entries and translations. IceWhale has separately developed ZimaOS, a more complete personal-cloud operating system evolved from CasaOS and built on a Debian base, positioned as a fuller alternative for users of Zima-brand hardware, though CasaOS itself remains the free, community-oriented project runnable on virtually any 64-bit Linux machine. CasaOS's target audience is home-lab enthusiasts, self-hosting hobbyists, and small teams who want an approachable, low-cost alternative to commercial NAS software or cloud storage subscriptions, trading the convenience of a managed service for full control over their own hardware and data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CasaOS is a free, open-source personal cloud operating system developed by IceWhale Technology, a company founded in 2020 and based in Shanghai, China. It originated as the pre-installed software for ZimaBoard, IceWhale's crowdfunded single-board server, before becoming a standalone open-source project that runs on almost any 64-bit Linux machine.

The project is hosted on GitHub under IceWhaleTech and has surpassed 33,000 stars, making it one of the most widely adopted home-server dashboards, alongside alternatives such as Umbrel and OpenMediaVault.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CasaOS runs on top of an existing Linux install (Ubuntu, Debian, Raspberry Pi OS) and provides a modern web dashboard for managing apps, files, and storage. Its one-click app store includes over 100 community-curated apps such as Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Home Assistant, Gitea, and AdGuard, each packaged as a Docker Compose-based 'CasaOS app.'

A built-in web file browser allows file management without needing SSH access, and storage tools visualize disk usage and let users mount external drives directly from the dashboard. Every installed app runs isolated inside its own Docker container.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CasaOS is entirely free and open-source, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees. Users only bear the cost of the hardware they choose to run it on, from a $35 Raspberry Pi to repurposed PCs or dedicated servers.

IceWhale Technology also sells ZimaBoard hardware and maintains ZimaOS, a more fully featured Debian-based personal cloud OS evolved from CasaOS, but CasaOS itself remains free for anyone to install on their own compatible hardware.

features.title: Broad Hardware Support
features.description: Runs on top of Ubuntu, Debian, or Raspberry Pi OS on hardware from a Raspberry Pi to repurposed laptops and servers.

features.title: Modern Dashboard UI
features.description: A clean, consumer-friendly web interface designed to make self-hosting accessible to non-experts.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Very approachable UI for non-technical self-hosters
Large, actively maintained one-click app catalog
Runs on inexpensive hardware, from a Raspberry Pi up
Large, active GitHub community with frequent updates
cons: Self-hosted, so users are responsible for their own updates and security
Smaller app catalog than some larger self-hosting platforms
Requires an existing Linux installation and basic technical comfort
Support is community-driven rather than backed by a commercial SLA

faqs.question: Is CasaOS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CasaOS is fully free and open-source with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What hardware does CasaOS run on?
faqs.answer: CasaOS runs on top of an existing Linux install and supports hardware ranging from a Raspberry Pi to repurposed laptops, PCs, and dedicated servers, including IceWhale's own ZimaBoard.

faqs.question: Who develops CasaOS?
faqs.answer: CasaOS is developed by IceWhale Technology, a company founded in 2020 and based in Shanghai, China, with contributions from an active open-source community.

faqs.question: What apps can I install with CasaOS?
faqs.answer: CasaOS's one-click app store includes over 100 community apps such as Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Home Assistant, Gitea, and AdGuard, each running in its own Docker container.

faqs.question: How is CasaOS different from ZimaOS?
faqs.answer: CasaOS is the free, open-source dashboard project that runs on any compatible Linux machine, while ZimaOS is a more complete Debian-based personal cloud operating system evolved from CasaOS, made by the same company for Zima-brand hardware.

enrichment.short_description: Casbin is a free, open-source authorization library that supports ACL, RBAC, and ABAC access control models across Go, Python, Java, Node.js, and more.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Casbin is an open-source access control library supporting ACL, RBAC, and ABAC models across Go, Python, Java, Node.js, and other languages. Free to use.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Casbin is an open-source authorization and access control library originally created in 2017 by Yang Luo, then a student at Peking University, and now maintained as an incubating project under the Apache Software Foundation (Apache Casbin, incubating). Rather than being a hosted service, Casbin is a code library that developers embed directly into their applications to handle permission checks, and it is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license at no cost. Its core idea is the PERM metamodel (Policy, Effect, Request, Matchers), which abstracts an access control model into a human-readable `.conf` configuration file. This lets a development team switch between access control paradigms — Access Control Lists (ACL), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), or combinations like RBAC with domains/tenants — by editing a model definition rather than rewriting application authorization logic. Policies (the actual rules, such as which role can access which resource) are stored separately from the model and can be loaded from files, relational or NoSQL databases, or custom adapters, and updated at runtime through Casbin's API without restarting the application. Casbin's runtime API supports common permission-management operations such as checking whether a given request is permitted, listing all roles assigned to a user, and adding or removing policies dynamically, which makes it suitable for applications that need to manage permissions programmatically rather than through static configuration alone. What distinguishes Casbin from many authorization libraries is its breadth of language support: the core implementation is in Go, with actively maintained ports for Python (pycasbin), Java (jCasbin), Node.js/JavaScript (node-casbin), .NET/C# (Casbin.NET), Rust (casbin-rs), Ruby, C/C++, and PHP, all published under the Apache organization on GitHub. This multi-language consistency allows organizations with polyglot codebases to apply the same access-control model and policy files across services written in different languages. The main Casbin repository has grown to more than 20,000 GitHub stars, reflecting broad production adoption across internet companies, SaaS products, and internal enterprise platforms. Casbin has no pricing or commercial tiers of its own since it is purely an open-source library; organizations that want a full identity and access management (IAM) product with a user interface, rather than just an authorization library, typically pair Casbin with a companion project such as Casdoor, which was built by the same original creator and uses Casbin as its underlying policy engine. Casbin is best suited for developers and platform teams who need fine-grained, model-flexible authorization logic embedded directly into an application or microservice, rather than teams looking for an out-of-the-box, UI-driven access management product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Casbin is an open-source authorization library created in 2017 by Yang Luo, then a student at Peking University, and now developed as an incubating project under the Apache Software Foundation. It is distributed free under the Apache 2.0 license and is embedded directly into applications as a code library rather than run as a hosted service.

Its core abstraction, the PERM metamodel, lets developers define an access control model in a configuration file and switch between paradigms like ACL, RBAC, and ABAC without rewriting application logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Casbin separates the access control model from the policy data, which can be stored in files, SQL/NoSQL databases, or custom adapters and updated at runtime via API calls. Its runtime API supports permission checks, role queries, and dynamic policy management.

The library is available across many languages — Go (core), Python, Java, Node.js, .NET, Rust, Ruby, C/C++, and PHP — all maintained under the Apache organization on GitHub, letting teams apply a consistent authorization model across polyglot codebases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Casbin is completely free and open-source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no commercial tiers, subscriptions, or paid support contracts offered by the project itself.

Teams that need a full identity and access management product with a management UI typically pair Casbin with a companion open-source project such as Casdoor, which uses Casbin as its underlying authorization engine.

features.title: Multiple Access Control Models
features.description: Supports ACL, RBAC, ABAC, and hybrid models like RBAC with domains/tenants via a single library.

features.title: PERM Metamodel Configuration
features.description: Access control models are defined in human-readable .conf files, decoupled from application code.

features.title: Multi-Language SDKs
features.description: Consistent implementations across Go, Python, Java, Node.js, .NET, Rust, Ruby, C/C++, and PHP.

features.title: Runtime Policy Management API
features.description: Add, remove, and query permissions and roles dynamically without restarting the application.

features.title: Pluggable Policy Storage
features.description: Policies can be stored in files or SQL/NoSQL databases through custom adapters.

features.title: Role Hierarchy Support
features.description: Native support for role inheritance and hierarchical permission structures.

pros: Framework- and language-agnostic authorization model usable across polyglot codebases
Mature and widely adopted in production, with 20,000+ GitHub stars on the core repository
Flexible enough to model ACL, RBAC, and ABAC without rewriting application logic
Backed by Apache Software Foundation incubation for open governance
Free and open source with no licensing cost
cons: Requires developers to learn its model/policy configuration syntax
No built-in user interface or hosted service on its own
Documentation depth varies somewhat across the different language SDKs
Fully self-managed, with no vendor support contract available

faqs.question: Is Casbin free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Casbin is fully open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to use in commercial and non-commercial projects.

faqs.question: What access control models does Casbin support?
faqs.answer: Casbin supports Access Control Lists (ACL), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), and hybrid models such as RBAC with domains, all defined through its PERM metamodel.

faqs.question: Which programming languages does Casbin support?
faqs.answer: Casbin's core is written in Go, with actively maintained ports for Python, Java, Node.js/JavaScript, .NET/C#, Rust, Ruby, C/C++, and PHP.

faqs.question: Who created Casbin?
faqs.answer: Casbin was created in 2017 by Yang Luo while at Peking University, and is now developed as an incubating project under the Apache Software Foundation.

faqs.question: Does Casbin include a user interface?
faqs.answer: No, Casbin is a library, not a hosted product with a UI. Projects that need a management interface typically pair it with a companion tool like Casdoor.

use_cases.title: Embedding fine-grained authorization
use_cases.description: Adding ACL, RBAC, or ABAC permission checks directly into an application without building custom access control logic from scratch.

use_cases.title: Standardizing access control across microservices
use_cases.description: Applying a consistent authorization model and policy set across services written in different languages.

enrichment.short_description: Casdoor is a free, open-source identity and access management (IAM) and SSO server supporting OAuth 2.0, OIDC, SAML, LDAP, WebAuthn, and MFA, self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Casdoor is a free, open-source IAM and SSO platform with a web UI, supporting OAuth 2.0, OIDC, SAML, LDAP, WebAuthn, and MFA for self-hosted deployment.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Casdoor is an open-source identity and access management (IAM) and single sign-on (SSO) platform created by Yang Luo, the same developer behind the Casbin authorization library, with the project originating around 2020 under the Casbin GitHub organization before being spun out into its own dedicated casdoor organization. It is licensed under Apache 2.0 and designed to be self-hosted, giving organizations a UI-first alternative to commercial identity providers such as Okta or Auth0. Unlike reverse-proxy-centric auth tools, Casdoor runs as a dedicated authentication and authorization server with a full web-based admin console for managing users, organizations, applications, and identity providers, rather than only sitting in front of existing services as a gatekeeper. Its protocol support is unusually broad for a single self-hosted project: OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC), SAML, CAS, LDAP, SCIM, WebAuthn, TOTP-based multi-factor authentication, and biometric options like Face ID, alongside integrations with more than 100 third-party identity providers, including Google, Azure AD/Entra ID, GitHub, and Google Workspace. Because Casdoor was built by Casbin's creator, it integrates natively with Casbin as its underlying authorization engine, letting administrators express fine-grained access policies (RBAC, ABAC, and more) on top of the authentication layer that Casdoor provides, rather than requiring a separate authorization system to be bolted on. As of 2026 the project has continued evolving toward supporting authentication for AI agents and tools, adding native Model Context Protocol (MCP) server support and OAuth 2.1 flows aimed at securing AI-agent-to-service authentication, positioning Casdoor as one of the first open-source IAM platforms to explicitly target agentic AI use cases in addition to traditional human user SSO. The main Casdoor repository has accumulated more than 11,000 GitHub stars. As with other self-hosted open-source infrastructure, Casdoor has no pricing tiers or subscription plans; it is free to deploy, and organizations bear only their own hosting and operational costs. It is typically compared against other self-hosted or open-source IAM options such as Keycloak, Authentik, and Zitadel, with Casdoor's main differentiators being its combined breadth of protocol support in a single binary, its UI-first admin experience, and its tight coupling with Casbin's authorization engine. Casdoor is best suited to engineering and platform teams that want to self-host SSO/IAM rather than pay for a commercial identity provider, and that are comfortable taking on the operational responsibility of running and securing an authentication server themselves, including applying updates and managing uptime, since there is no vendor SLA behind the free, community-maintained project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Casdoor is an open-source identity and access management (IAM) and single sign-on (SSO) platform created around 2020 by Yang Luo, the same developer behind the Casbin authorization library. It began under the Casbin GitHub organization before moving to its own dedicated casdoor organization, and is licensed under Apache 2.0 for free, self-hosted deployment.

Rather than acting only as a reverse-proxy gatekeeper in front of existing apps, Casdoor runs as a dedicated auth server with a full web-based admin console for managing users, organizations, applications, and identity providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Casdoor supports a broad set of authentication protocols in one self-hosted deployment: OAuth 2.0, OIDC, SAML, CAS, LDAP, SCIM, WebAuthn, TOTP-based MFA, and Face ID, along with integrations for 100+ third-party identity providers including Google, Azure AD, and GitHub.

Because it was built by Casbin's creator, Casdoor integrates natively with Casbin for fine-grained authorization (RBAC, ABAC) on top of authentication. More recent development has added native Model Context Protocol (MCP) server support and OAuth 2.1 flows for authenticating AI agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Casdoor is free and open-source with no pricing tiers or subscription plans; it is self-hosted, so the only costs organizations incur are their own hosting and operational overhead.
There is no official vendor support contract or SLA — support comes from the open-source community and project maintainers, similar to other self-hosted IAM tools like Keycloak and Authentik.

features.title: Web-Based Admin Console
features.description: A UI-first console for managing users, organizations, applications, and identity providers.

features.title: OAuth 2.0 & OIDC Provider

features.title: SAML, CAS & LDAP Support
features.description: Enterprise protocol support for integrating with legacy and corporate directory systems.

features.title: WebAuthn, TOTP & MFA
features.description: Passwordless and multi-factor authentication options, including biometric Face ID login.

features.title: 100+ Identity Provider Integrations
features.description: Pre-built connectors for Google, Azure AD/Entra ID, GitHub, Google Workspace, and more.

features.title: Casbin-Powered Authorization
features.description: Built-in fine-grained access control (RBAC/ABAC) using Casbin as the underlying policy engine.

features.title: AI Agent Authentication (MCP/OAuth 2.1)
features.description: Native Model Context Protocol server support and OAuth 2.1 flows for authenticating AI agents and tools.

pros: Broad protocol support (OAuth, OIDC, SAML, CAS, LDAP) in a single self-hosted deployment
Tight, native integration with Casbin for fine-grained authorization
Actively developed, including newer AI-agent authentication support via MCP/OAuth 2.1
Free and open source under Apache 2.0
UI-first design makes administration more approachable than many developer-only IAM tools
cons: Self-hosting requires the team to own its own operations and security
Smaller ecosystem and community than commercial IAM vendors like Okta or Auth0
No official vendor support contract or SLA
Documentation and tooling are geared more toward developers than non-technical admins

faqs.question: Is Casdoor free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Casdoor is free and open-source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no pricing tiers or subscription fees; users only pay for their own hosting.

faqs.question: What authentication protocols does Casdoor support?
faqs.answer: Casdoor supports OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC), SAML, CAS, LDAP, SCIM, WebAuthn, TOTP-based MFA, and Face ID, plus 100+ third-party identity provider integrations.

faqs.question: How does Casdoor relate to Casbin?
faqs.answer: Casdoor was created by the same developer as the Casbin authorization library and uses Casbin natively as its underlying policy engine for fine-grained access control.

faqs.question: Can Casdoor authenticate AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, Casdoor has added native Model Context Protocol (MCP) server support and OAuth 2.1 flows aimed at authenticating AI agents and tools, in addition to traditional human user SSO.

faqs.question: Is Casdoor a hosted service or self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Casdoor is self-hosted; organizations deploy and manage their own instance rather than using a managed cloud service.

use_cases.audience: Engineering teams wanting to avoid per-user IAM licensing costs

use_cases.title: Fine-grained access control on top of authentication
use_cases.description: Combining Casdoor's authentication layer with Casbin's RBAC/ABAC authorization engine for detailed permission management.
use_cases.audience: Platform teams building internal or customer-facing applications

use_cases.title: AI agent authentication
use_cases.description: Authenticating AI agents and tools via MCP and OAuth 2.1 rather than only human users.
use_cases.audience: Teams building AI agent or MCP-based integrations
